summary: DHH celebrates Omarchy, his new Linux distribution, hitting a petabyte of downloads (about 150,000 installs) in its first month. He argues this explosive growth represents genuine product-market fit, driven by Apple and Microsoft alienating developers and computer enthusiasts through forced obsolescence, AI bloat, and declining software quality. He makes the case that Linux has never been more viable as a desktop OS because web apps have replaced most native software that once kept people tethered to Windows or Mac. Omarchy succeeds by packaging Arch Linux and the Hyprland window manager into an opinionated but fully customizable distribution that removes the traditional gatekeeping around Linux setup.
key_points: Omarchy grew faster than any open-source project DHH has been involved with, including Ruby on Rails, reaching 150,000 installs in its first month
Apple and Microsoft are driving users away by cutting off older hardware, forcing AI features, and declining in software quality
The historic gap in native GUI apps that kept people on Windows/Mac has nearly closed because the web has become the dominant computing platform
Linux offers superior developer performance — a $500 Beelink Mini PC can compete with an M4 Max machine costing thousands for real-world workloads
Omarchy's appeal lies in combining preconfigured usability with full user agency, rejecting both corporate lock-in and Linux gatekeeping culture
money_quotes: This is what product-market fit looks like. Doesn't matter if the product is free or not. The fit is obvious.
Microsoft is killing off Windows 10, which in turn cuts off a whole slew of perfectly fine computers made prior to around 2017–2018.
A Mac Mini sold as recently as 2023 is now end-of-life!
The historic gap in native GUI apps has never mattered less either. The web has conquered all as the dominant computing platform.
In the past, missing, say, Photoshop was a big deal. Now it's Figma — a web app! — that's driving designers.
The argument that owning your computer, fully and deeply, is starting to resonate.

All it took was someone to actually put all the pieces together, ignore the Linux neckbeards who insist you aren't worthy to run Arch or Hyprland without spending a hundred hours setting it up from scratch, and invite everyone to the party!
key_insight: Apple and Microsoft's tightening grip on their platforms has created a historic opening for Linux, and Omarchy proves that packaging an opinionated, beautiful experience — rather than demanding users earn their way in — is what mainstream Linux adoption actually needed.
